revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==

The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|12=draft}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=draft}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|12=draft}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=draft}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=draft}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=draft}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神|12=draft}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=draft}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|12=draft}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|12=draft}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=draft}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|12=draft}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=draft}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|12=draft}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=draft}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|12=draft}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=draft}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|12=draft}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=draft}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|12=draft}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=draft}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=draft}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神|12=draft}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=draft}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|12=draft}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|12=draft}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=draft}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|12=draft}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=draft}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|12=draft}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=draft}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine] <!--Okada Shrine--> <!--Okada Shrine-->

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|12=draft|12=draft}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=draft|12=draft}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=draft|12=draft}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|12=draft|12=draft}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|12=draft|12=draft}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|12=draft|12=draft}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|12=draft|12=draft}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=draft|12=draft}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|12=draft|12=draft}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=draft|12=draft}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

revision.comment: 22 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:岡田宮 up to rev 102820197
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 岡田宮 |画像 = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] |所在地 = [[福岡県]][[北九州市]][[八幡西区]]岡田町1-1 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = 神日本磐余彦命（神武天皇） |社格 = 村社 |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = 岡田神社 |例祭 = 10月18日～19日 |神事 = |}} '''岡田宮'''（おかだぐう）は、[[福岡県]][[北九州市]][[八幡西区]][[黒崎 (北九州市)|黒崎]]地区にある[[神社]]で、正式には'''岡田神社'''。
== 概要 == [[古事記]]に[[神武天皇|初代天皇神武天皇]]が[[神武東征|東征]]の折に逗留したと記載されている古社で、天・地・人の三ノ宮を有する。
毎年七月に岡田宮へ奉納する[[黒崎祇園山笠]]は北九州の風物詩として有名である。
== 祭神 == * 岡田宮：神日本磐余彦命（[[神武天皇]]） * 熊手宮：[[大国主|大国主命]]、[[スクナビコナ|少名彦神]]、[[熊鰐|県主熊鰐命]] * 八所宮：[[タカミムスビ|高皇産霊神]]、[[カミムスビ|神皇産霊神]]、玉留産霊神、[[生産霊神]]、足産霊神、[[大宮売神]]、[[事代主|事代主神]]、[[御食津神|御膳神]]
== 歴史 == かつて崗地方（旧[[遠賀郡]]）を治めた熊族が洞海菊竹ノ浜（貞元）に祖先神を祀ったのが始まりとされ、そのためにこの地域一帯を『熊手』と号したといわれる。後、[[神武天皇]]が東征（[[神武東征]]）の途上に、この地に1年間逗留し八所神を祀ったとされ、神武東征にある岡田宮の候補地の一つである。
[[仲哀天皇]]の時代には恭順した崗県主[[熊鰐]]の案内によって、[[神功皇后]]が岡田宮の八所神を奉祭したとの記事が[[日本書紀]]に載る。
岡田宮の鎮座する地は北部九州の要衝の地であり、[[天慶]]3年[[藤原純友]]の乱の際には官軍の主将・[[小野好古]]と副将・[[源経基]]が必勝を祈願し神鈴を奉納したと伝わり、[[建久]]5年に遠賀郡・鞍手郡を領した[[宇都宮重業]]は祭礼法度を定め、波多野兼直に祭祀させたという。以来、社領18ヶ所・末寺9坊と栄えた。[[永禄]]2年に大友氏の兵火に遭い社殿を焼失したものの、同8年に[[麻生元重]]により社殿が復興される。
[[慶長]]10年、[[黒崎城]]が築城され黒崎宿が整備されると貞元から現在地へ遷座され、黒崎熊手の総鎮守として[[福岡藩]]はもとより[[長崎街道]]参勤の諸大名、上り下りの文人墨客より篤い崇敬を受けた。[[慶応]]元年には[[三条実美]]が太宰府へ落ち延びる最中に和歌を奉納している。
[[明治]]5年に村社に列し、同41年には神饌幣帛料供進社に指定される。現在では[[北九州市]]の副都心である黒崎地区の鎮守神の一社として多くの参詣者で賑わう。
== 祭事・年中行事 == * 祇園大祭（黒崎祇園山笠）：7月21日～23日 * 放生大祭：9月24日～25日 * 秋季大祭（例大祭）：10月18日～19日
== 交通 == * [[九州旅客鉄道|JR]][[鹿児島本線]] [[黒崎駅]]から徒歩で約10分(820m)。 * [[北九州高速4号線]][[黒崎出入口]]より約3㎞。参拝者用無料駐車場あり
== 外部リンク == * [https://okadagu.jp 岡田宮] *
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかたくう}} [[Category:福岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:北九州市の神社]] [[Category:八幡西区の歴史]] [[Category:八幡西区の建築物]]

毎年七月に岡田宮へ奉納する[[黒崎祇園山笠]]は北九州の風物詩として有名である。

== 歴史 == かつて崗地方（旧[[遠賀郡]]）を治めた熊族が洞海菊竹ノ浜（貞元）に祖先神を祀ったのが始まりとされ、そのためにこの地域一帯を『熊手』と号したといわれる。後、[[神武天皇]]が東征（[[神武東征]]）の途上に、この地に1年間逗留し八所神を祀ったとされ、神武東征にある岡田宮の候補地の一つである。
[[仲哀天皇]]の時代には恭順した崗県主[[熊鰐]]の案内によって、[[神功皇后]]が岡田宮の八所神を奉祭したとの記事が[[日本書紀]]に載る。
岡田宮の鎮座する地は北部九州の要衝の地であり、[[天慶]]3年[[藤原純友]]の乱の際には官軍の主将・[[小野好古]]と副将・[[源経基]]が必勝を祈願し神鈴を奉納したと伝わり、[[建久]]5年に遠賀郡・鞍手郡を領した[[宇都宮重業]]は祭礼法度を定め、波多野兼直に祭祀させたという。以来、社領18ヶ所・末寺9坊と栄えた。[[永禄]]2年に大友氏の兵火に遭い社殿を焼失したものの、同8年に[[麻生元重]]により社殿が復興される。
[[慶長]]10年、[[黒崎城]]が築城され黒崎宿が整備されると貞元から現在地へ遷座され、黒崎熊手の総鎮守として[[福岡藩]]はもとより[[長崎街道]]参勤の諸大名、上り下りの文人墨客より篤い崇敬を受けた。[[慶応]]元年には[[三条実美]]が太宰府へ落ち延びる最中に和歌を奉納している。
[[明治]]5年に村社に列し、同41年には神饌幣帛料供進社に指定される。現在では[[北九州市]]の副都心である黒崎地区の鎮守神の一社として多くの参詣者で賑わう。
== 祭事・年中行事 == * 祇園大祭（黒崎祇園山笠）：7月21日～23日 * 放生大祭：9月24日～25日 * 秋季大祭（例大祭）：10月18日～19日
== 交通 == * [[九州旅客鉄道|JR]][[鹿児島本線]] [[黒崎駅]]から徒歩で約10分(820m)。 * [[北九州高速4号線]][[黒崎出入口]]より約3㎞。参拝者用無料駐車場あり

{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかたくう}} [[Category:福岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:北九州市の神社]] [[Category:八幡西区の歴史]] [[Category:八幡西区の建築物]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine] {{draft categories|
}}

This area was later named "Kumate." During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|12=draft|12=draft}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=draft|12=draft}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|12=draft|12=draft}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=draft|12=draft}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==

Free parking is available for visitors.

* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ikumusubi|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|qq=draft|qq=draft}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagoshima Main Line|qq=}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahatanishi-ku|qq=}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu Tennō|qq=}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|qq=}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahatanishi-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jinmu|qq=}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|qq=}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga District|qq=}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Chūai|qq=}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Tsunemoto|qq=}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagoshima Main Line|qq=}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
