* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|qq=}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga District|qq=}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|lt=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Chūai|qq=}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Tsunemoto|qq=}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagoshima Main Line|qq=}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahatanishi-ku|qq=}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Jinmu Tennō|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu Tennō|qq=}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|qq=}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|ja|八幡西区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yahatanishi-ku|qq=}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|ja|北九州市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu|qq=}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|ja|福岡県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukuoka Prefecture|qq=}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Jinmu|qq=}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|lt=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|ja|神武天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|qq=}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|qq=}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District|ja|遠賀郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onga District|qq=}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|lt=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|ja|神武東征|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|qq=}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|ja|仲哀天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Chūai|qq=}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|ja|源経基|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Tsunemoto|qq=}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnin War|qq=}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|ja|黒崎駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Station|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|ja|鹿児島本線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagoshima Main Line|qq=}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Jinmu Tennō]] (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.

In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.

* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19

The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Jinmu Tennō]] (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19

Free parking is available for visitors.

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Shinto shrines in Kitakyushu
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Jinmu Tennō]] (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11473513]] Wikidata name: Okagagū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Okada guu.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|33|51|43|N|130|46|03|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Jinmu Tennō]] (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|qq=}} area of [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|qq=|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|qq=|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|qq=|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|qq=|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Kamuyamato Iwarebiko]] (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]], {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiyanome|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiyanome|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former [[:en:Onga District, Fukuoka|Onga District]]) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]], the local ruler Kumawani guided [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]].
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and [[:en:Minamoto no Tsunemoto|Minamoto no Tsunemoto]] prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]] in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from [[:en:Kurosaki Station|Kurosaki Station]] on the [[:en:Kagoshima Main Line|Kagoshima Main Line]]. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|1=Kurosaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678156}} area of [[:en:Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|Yahatanishi-ku]], [[:en:Kitakyushu|Kitakyushu]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]]. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] as a place where [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jinmu]], Japan's first emperor, stayed during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]]. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his [[:en:Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|Jinmu's Eastern Expedition]] and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.

Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jinmu}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarebiko}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
