'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|1=Kurosaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678156}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jinmu}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarebiko}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District, Fukuoka|en|Onga District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1156362|lt=Onga District}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|en|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|WD=Q3099236|lt=Minamoto no Tsunemoto}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|en|Kurosaki Station|WD=Q871645|lt=Kurosaki Station}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|en|Kurosaki Station|WD=Q871645|lt=Kurosaki Station}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Okada guu.JPG | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|33.861944|N|130.7675|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jinmu Tennō}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|WD=Q1044377}} }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|1=Kurosaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678156}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jinmu}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarebiko}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District, Fukuoka|en|Onga District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1156362|lt=Onga District}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|en|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|WD=Q3099236|lt=Minamoto no Tsunemoto}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|en|Kurosaki Station|WD=Q871645|lt=Kurosaki Station}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarebiko}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District, Fukuoka|en|Onga District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1156362|lt=Onga District}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|en|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|WD=Q3099236|lt=Minamoto no Tsunemoto}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|en|Kurosaki Station|WD=Q871645|lt=Kurosaki Station}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Kurosaki Interchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]

'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|1=Kurosaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678156}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku|1=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu|1=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jinmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.

In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. * A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|en|Kurosaki Station|WD=Q871645|lt=Kurosaki Station|1=Kurosaki Station}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line|1=Kagoshima Main Line}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Q1956489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Okada Shrine (岡田宮) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Okada guu.JPG | location = {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu|1=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku|1=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, Okada Town 1-1 | coordinates = {{Coord|33.861944|N|130.7675|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Jinmu Tennō|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (Kamuyamato Iwarebiko) | Shrine Rank = Village shrine (村社) | consecration_year = Unknown | Main hall = | Alternate name = Okada Shrine (岡田神社) | festival = October 18–19 | Rituals = | location = {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku|WD=Q1044377|1=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}} }}
'''Okada Shrine''' (岡田宮) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the {{ill|Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|ja|黒崎 (北九州市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki (Kitakyushu)|1=Kurosaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678156}} area of {{ill|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|en|Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū|WD=Q1044377|lt=Yahatanishi-ku|1=Yahatanishi-ku, Kitakyūshū}}, {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|WD=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu|1=Kitakyushu}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}. Its formal name is '''Okada Shrine''' (岡田神社).
== Overview ==
The shrine is an ancient establishment mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} as a place where {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jinmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, Japan's first emperor, stayed during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}}. It is notable for having three shrines dedicated to heaven, earth, and humanity.
The {{ill|Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|ja|黒崎祇園山笠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa|1=Kurosaki Gion Yamagasa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11678183}}, an annual festival dedicated to Okada Shrine every July, is well-known as a cultural highlight of Kitakyushu.
== Enshrined Deities ==
* '''Okada Shrine''': {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Kamuyamato Iwarebiko|1=Emperor Jimmu}} (Emperor Jinmu) * '''Kumate Shrine''': {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunahikona|ja|少名彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunahikona|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and Kumawani (熊鰐命) * '''Hasshinden''': {{ill|Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|lt=Takami-Musubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ|1=Takamimusubi}}, {{ill|Kami-Musubi|ja|神皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kami-Musubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3275901}}, Tamatsume-Musubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|ja|生産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikumusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天活玉命}}, Tarumusubi-no-Kami, {{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|WD=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miketsu-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== History ==
The shrine is believed to have been established by the Kumate clan, who ruled the Ōnga region (former {{ill|Onga District, Fukuoka|en|Onga District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1156362|lt=Onga District|1=Onga District, Fukuoka}}) and worshiped their ancestral deities at the present-day Deguchi-Kikutake-no-Hama (Sadamoto). This area was later named "Kumate." According to tradition, Emperor Jinmu stayed here for a year during his {{ill|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|en|Jimmu's Eastern Expedition|WD=Q11590444|lt=Jinmu's Eastern Expedition|1=Jimmu's Eastern Expedition}} and enshrined the eight deities of the Hasshinden, making it one of the candidate sites mentioned in the Jinmu Expedition.
During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Chūai|en|Emperor Chūai|WD=Q179971|lt=Emperor Chūai|1=Emperor Chūai}}, the local ruler Kumawani guided {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} to enshrine the Hasshinden deities at Okada Shrine, as noted in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}.
In later years, Okada Shrine became an important religious site in northern Kyushu. During the {{ill|Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja|藤原純友の乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Sumitomo Rebellion|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 承平天慶の乱|1=Tengyō no Ran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2975322}} in 940 CE, generals {{ill|Ono no Yoshikō|ja|小野好古|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ono no Yoshikō|1=Ono no Yoshifuru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11463979}} and {{ill|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|en|Minamoto no Tsunemoto|WD=Q3099236|lt=Minamoto no Tsunemoto|1=Minamoto no Tsunemoto}} prayed for victory and dedicated a sacred bell to the shrine. The shrine prospered during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} under the patronage of {{ill|Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja|宇都宮重業|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya Shigezane|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who instituted rules for its rituals.
Despite destruction by fire during the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1560, it was rebuilt by {{ill|Asō Motoshige|ja|麻生元重|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asō Motoshige|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the shrine was relocated to its current site, becoming the guardian shrine of the Kurosaki region and receiving widespread reverence.
== Festivals and Events ==
* '''Gion Festival (Kurosaki Gion Yamakasa)''': July 21–23 * '''Hōjō Festival''': September 24–25 * '''Autumn Grand Festival (festival)''': October 18–19
== Access ==
* A 10-minute walk (820m) from {{ill|Kurosaki Station|en|Kurosaki Station|WD=Q871645|lt=Kurosaki Station|1=Kurosaki Station}} on the {{ill|Kagoshima Main Line|en|Kagoshima Main Line|WD=Q1187520|lt=Kagoshima Main Line|1=Kagoshima Main Line}}. * Approximately 3 km from {{ill|Kurosaki Interchange|ja|黒崎出入口|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurosaki Interchange|1=Q1956489|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1956489}} on the {{ill|Kitakyushu Expressway|ja|北九州高速道路|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitakyushu Expressway|1=Kitakyushu highway|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2260027}}. Free parking is available for visitors.
== External Links ==
* [https://okadagu.jp Official Website of Okada Shrine]
